ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster. FOODON_03311737 (lobster food product) is the closest parent class covering cooked lobster preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers lobster as a commodity. No specific code exists for the Singapore chili sauce preparation; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 covers lobster as a food substance. No specific code for Singapore chili slipper lobster dish exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster is approximately 300 g, comprising half a slipper lobster with a generous portion of chili tomato egg-drop sauce, served with mantou (fried buns) on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab (Singapore-style) — nutrient profile adapted for slipper lobster; USDA FoodData Central: Spiny lobster, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175183) used as crustacean base; sauce macros estimated from Singapore HPB Foodo chilli crab sauce component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutrient entry exists for Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FDC cooked spiny lobster (FDC ID 175183) as the crustacean base, blended with sauce-component estimates derived from HPB Singapore Foodo chilli crab data. The slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) has a nutritional profile closely analogous to spiny lobster. Sauce contribution (chili, tomato, egg, oil, sugar) adds approximately 15–20 kcal per 100 g to the base crustacean value. Final per-100g values represent the whole dish as served (lobster flesh plus sauce), consistent with HPB chilli crab methodology.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood dishes with sauce — analogous estimate based on chilli crab and similar crustacean-in-sauce preparations; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed seafood-in-sauce dishes. The dish is predominantly protein and fat (lobster flesh) with a moderate-carbohydrate chili tomato sauce containing sugar and cornstarch. Protein and fat substantially blunt glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database lists mixed seafood dishes and tomato-based sauces in the low GI range (GI 30–45). GI 35 is a conservative mid-point estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.6) / 100 = 5.46, rounded to 6.1 accounting for sauce carbohydrate contribution at 300 g serving (5.2 g carb per 100 g × 3 = 15.6 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1440 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced-sauce servings, as the chili tomato sauce is the primary source of sodium, contributing approximately 1440 mg per standard 300 g restaurant portion — exceeding 60% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from chili sauce (sugar, cornstarch) with low GI mitigated by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster has a low estimated GI of 35 and a moderate glycemic load of approximately 6 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake including the accompanying mantou buns, which significantly increase the meal's glycemic load, and prefer smaller portions of sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g), partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from lobster flesh
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While slipper lobster provides lean protein and beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s, the high sodium content of the chili sauce poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit restaurant portions to 150 g, avoid the high-sodium sauce, and note that the lobster flesh itself is a heart-healthy protein source when prepared with minimal added salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster is a premium Singaporean seafood dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis), a flat-bodied crustacean distinct from clawed lobsters, cooked in the iconic Singapore chili sauce. The dish is a variation of the celebrated Singapore Chili Crab, substituting slipper lobster for mud crab. The slipper lobster is typically halved, wok-fried until the shell is fragrant, then simmered in a rich, semi-thick sauce made from blended red chilies, tomato sauce, garlic, ginger, shallots, soy sauce, sugar, and egg, thickened with cornstarch. The result is a dish that is simultaneously spicy, sweet, savory, and umami-rich, with a glossy, clingy sauce that coats the tender, slightly sweet lobster flesh. Texturally, the lobster meat is firm yet succulent, and the sauce has a velvety, egg-drop consistency. The dish is a staple of Zi Char (煮炒) restaurants and upscale seafood houses in Singapore, often served with deep-fried or steamed mantou buns for dipping. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with significant sodium from the sauce. The lobster flesh contributes lean protein (~14.5 g per 100 g), omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc. The chili sauce adds carbohydrates from sugar and cornstarch, as well as lycopene from tomato. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without the accompanying mantou buns.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Singapore Chili Slipper Lobster exists in any approved primary source (HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central). Values are adapted from USDA FDC cooked spiny lobster (FDC ID 175183) as the closest approved crustacean analogue, with sauce macros estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo chilli crab component methodology. The slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) is nutritionally analogous to spiny lobster. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates: USDA FDC cooked spiny lobster (FDC ID 175183) used as crustacean base; sauce contribution estimated from HPB Foodo chilli crab data. No exact match exists in any approved primary source for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC source entry for cooked spiny lobster. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC source entry for cooked spiny lobster. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero: crustaceans and vegetable-oil-based sauces do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FDC data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for mixed seafood-in-sauce dishes; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI literature. 6. The cuisine tag in the input JSON was 'Malaysian'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as Singapore Chili Crab/Lobster is a canonical Singaporean dish, though it is popular in Malaysian Zi Char restaurants. The cuisine_tags from the input (Malaysian, Crab, Zi Char) are preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is listed as true as slipper lobster is generally considered halal in the Shafi'i school followed in Singapore; however, individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. The price data from the input (SGD 42.00) is not included in the RESULT template as it is not a supported field.
